subject: Public Health
title: Fluoride in Drinking Water
description: A micro-lesson that makes Fluoride in Drinking Water usable.

hook.text: In 1945, Grand Rapids, Michigan became the first city to add fluoride to water—cutting cavities by 60%.

buildup.text: Fluoride strengthens tooth enamel and prevents decay—the CDC calls water fluoridation a top health achievement.

discovery.text: Community water fluoridation reaches everyone equally—regardless of income, education, or dental access.

twist.text: Excessive natural fluoride in groundwater causes fluorosis—damaging teeth and bones in parts of Africa and Asia.

climax.text: The optimal level is narrow—enough to prevent cavities but not enough to cause fluorosis.

punchline.text: The dose makes the medicine. Too little or too much, and it fails.

quiz.question: By how much did water fluoridation reduce cavities in Grand Rapids?
